Alas, a settlement alone won=E2=80=99t ensure immunity from new antitrust c=
hallenges. To address this, Kessler and cohort Steve Berman are now talking=

ereby athletes will have an annual opportunity to express any grievances be=
fore a judge. Alas, I know many in the antitrust field who are skeptical at=

re litigation. With billions buying a semblance of peace in the college spo=

eral law, perhaps, or collective bargaining. Time will tell. =E2=80=94Eriq =

Under Sean McManus=E2=80=99s nearly three-decade tenure, CBS Sports was kno=
wn for stability, boasting the longest and deepest relationships in the bus=
iness. And as I=E2=80=99ve noted in the past, this was also the leitmotif o=
f McManus=E2=80=99s drama-free succession plan. David Berson, who ascended =
to the top job last month after his old boss=E2=80=99s retirement, had been=
 McManus=E2=80=99s heir apparent for some 13 years, fully ensconced in one =
of the industry=E2=80=99s great partnerships.

But as Berson prepared for his first town hall meeting as president and C.E=
=2EO., he realized that this stability=20=
now seemed uncertain. Staffers were un=
derstandably anxious about Shari Redstone=E2=80=99s enigmatic auction proce=
ss for Paramount Global, the future of the linear business, and the increas=
ingly exorbitant market for live sports rights. During a one-hour
presentation, Berson reminded his 600 or so charges that they=E2=80=99d nee=
d to remain focused on what they could control while staying aggressive in =
the rights landscape. =E2=80=9CThe message was continuity and stability, bu=
t with eyes wide open on all the challenges that we all face as part of thi=
s changing industry,=E2=80=9D Berson told me about two weeks later, over lu=
nch at a quiet Italian restaurant on West 57th Street, his first interview =
in his new job. =E2=80=9CWe have all of our marquee rights locked up for a =
long time. But the fans of today consume stuff very differently from the fa=
ns of yesterday, so we need to make sure that our brand and our content is =
as relevant for those fans moving forward as it has always been.=E2=80=9D

I=E2=80=99ve known Berson for about a quarter-century, starting when he was=
 a grunt at ESPN. He=E2=80=99s always been a pensive and thoughtful guy, es=
chewing the limelight, and always happy to defer credit and attention. Last=
 week, industry insiders took note when Berson
allowed E.V.P. Dan Weinberg to be quoted in news articles about the NFL sch=
edule. In the old days, McManus believed CBS Sports should speak with one v=
oice, and he had always been that voice.

Obviously, the defining story around Paramount these days is about whether =
Redstone will sell to David Ellison and RedBird or Apollo and Sony, or some=
 other mystery bidder. But when I pressed Berson on this topic multiple tim=
es=E2=80=94and, in particular, the difficulty of leading a sports group ami=
d so much chaos at the parentco level=E2=80=94he elegantly demurred. Instea=
d, he focused on the strength of CBS Sports, which is locked into long-term=
 deals with the NFL, Big Ten, March Madness, UEFA, PGA Tour, and the Master=
s. Despite the angst surrounding Shari=E2=80=99s next move, CBS produced th=
e most watched program in television history with February=E2=80=99s Super =
Bowl; racked up the largest NFL audience in 25 years; and broadcast the Fin=
al Four, the Masters and the PGA
Championship. =E2=80=9COur folks understand the importance of what they do,=
 and people have focused on what we can control and the important role our =
content plays in the company and for sports fans,=E2=80=9D Berson said.

It=E2=80=99s a message that resonates throughout the network. Sources have =
told me that the length of CBS Sports=E2=80=99 NFL deal=E2=80=94it has a de=
cade left, although the NFL can opt out in seven years=E2=80=94is one of th=
e predominant reasons investors are kicking the tires on Paramount.

Hope & Change

Berson finds it extraordinarily difficult to discuss even small changes tha=
t will come now that he=E2=80=99s in charge of CBS Sports. Berson and McMan=
us remain close, and he is careful not to reveal areas where he disagreed e=
ven slightly with his former boss. In truth, though, Berson bought into McM=
anus=E2=80=99s world view on sports media and insists that he will carry it=
 forward. Under McManus, CBS focused on producing marquee events, opting to=
 stay away from random regular season games, and Berson doesn=E2=80=99t pla=
n to move off that strategy. =E2=80=9CThat=E2=80=99s what CBS Sports has be=
en for generations,=E2=80=9D Berson told me. =E2=80=9CWe=E2=80=99re very, v=
ery focused on big events that cut through and matter and deliver massive a=
udiences, and our rights portfolio is aligned to deliver on that.=E2=80=9D

You=E2=80=99re most likely to see Berson=E2=80=99s influence in digital and=
 social, where CBS
Sports will take a few more risks, display more of a personality, and promo=
te a stronger CBS Sports-branded voice. You=E2=80=99ll even see it come out=
 in some of the music and graphics the network uses. And we=E2=80=99ve alre=
ady seen some intimation of his taste and perspective via his decision not =
to not renew the contracts of Boomer Esiason and Phil Simms, longtime mains=
tays on The NFL Today. In their place, Berson brought in Matt Ryan and rene=
wed star newcomer from last year J.J. Watt. I suggested that these moves bo=
re his signature, but Berson replied that the show would have undergone som=
e changes even if McManus were still around.

Continuity with change=E2=80=94that old spoofy Selina Meyer slogan from Vee=
p=E2=80=94is an apt description of the Berson housekeeping remit. He replac=
ed two legendary quarterbacks with a pair of younger retired All Pros and i=
s leaving the rest of the roundtable in place. Nate Burleson even emerged
during the middle of the town hall to interview Berson onstage, playfully r=
ibbing him about his sartorial choices (those quarter zips) and his avuncul=
ar command of Gen Z tastes (he kidded him about his overemphasized pronunci=
ation of TikTok). =E2=80=9CWe really just talked about having a standard of=
 excellence in everything we do,=E2=80=9D Berson said. =E2=80=9CMy strong h=
ope and desire is for us to continue to push to get better and take chances=
=2E We want quality and we want risk.=20=
And I want to foster a culture that=E2=
=80=99s about being respectful and being classy and being good teammates wh=
ile setting a high bar.=E2=80=9D

bate that I=E2=80=99d love for you to weigh in on. Is Inside the NBA a good=
 basketball show, or is it cable=E2=80=99s most successful late night show?=
 My co-worker feels it=E2=80=99s not truly a basketball show because you do=
n=E2=80=99t walk away from it knowing more about the game. I argue it=E2=80=
=99s a basketball show, but that it=E2=80=99s built for a midnight/1 a.m. a=
udience that is used to being entertained
as much as taught.=E2=80=9D =E2=80=94A Varsity subscriber
[Ed note: If I only get those two choices, I=E2=80=99ll agree with your co-=
worker. It=E2=80=99s an entertainment show. In reality, it falls right in b=
etween, which is why it=E2=80=99s been so hard for ESPN to figure out how t=
o compete with it.]

On the NFL and Netflix: =E2=80=9CNetflix=E2=80=99s NFL deal has nothing to =
do with acquiring subs or mitigating churn domestically. It has everything =
to do with wising up on the R.O.I. of their content. Matt Belloni had The I=
rishman production budget at $225M, exclusive of marketing, so let's say $3=
00M. Two NFL games for half the price of a shitty movie is objectively a go=
od deal. =E2=80=A6 And most of that return will come from ads. Netflix comf=
ortably has scale over everyone in terms of subs and time spent. But ~all o=
f their subs are ad-free! Live sports unlocks all of that because it allows=
 them to serve the =E2=80=98ad-free=E2=80=99 members with ads=E2=80=94pre- =
and postgame, timeouts,
halftime=E2=80=94and it carries a traditional B2B benefit, too. They can bu=
ndle the ad sales like the old days=E2=80=94Sure, Liberty Insurance, you wa=
nt a spot on our Christmas Day game? Well, also buy X, Y, and Z!=E2=80=9D =
=E2=80=94A data analyst

More on the NFL and Netflix: =E2=80=9CHave you heard anything about who=E2=
=80=99s handling production for the NFL Netflix Christmas games?=E2=80=9D =
=E2=80=94A production company executive who wants business.
[Ed note: This is one of the great unanswered questions from the Netflix-NF=
L deal. I=E2=80=99m working on it.]

Still more on the NFL and Netflix: =E2=80=9CWith Netflix acquiring Christma=
s Day NFL games, I=E2=80=99m curious how this will shake out for commercial=
 video customers.=E2=80=9D =E2=80=94A digital content and social media exec=
utive
[Ed note: This is another of the great unanswered questions from the Netfli=
x-NFL deal.]

On NFL media rights: =E2=80=9CRegarding your insight into the NFL=E2=80=99s=
 future
TV/streaming rights deal a decade from now: It will no doubt not be in a de=
cade, when the current deal expires after 2033, but rather when the NFL und=
oubtedly decides to opt out early in seven years. The legacy companies like=
 Fox and CBS will find their current deal a bargain compared to the ransom =
they will have to pay once that opt-out is triggered.=E2=80=9D =E2=80=94A b=
roadcaster

Everyone is an editor: =E2=80=9CI=E2=80=99d love to see more coverage on th=
e slow-motion car wreck that is the PGA/PIF negotiations. It=E2=80=99s quit=
e bad, probably worse than reported. No major sport, especially one whose a=
udience is aging faster than the others, has done more damage to its brand =
in such a short time than men=E2=80=99s professional golf. The fact that th=
e players, who are smart men but clearly in over their heads, are driving t=
he decision is insane. There=E2=80=99s a reason the NBA didn=E2=80=99t ask =
LeBron to negotiate its rights deals. It=E2=80=99s a huge issue, or as your=
 eloquent editor
Jon Kelly likes to say, it=E2=80=99s one of the stories of our time.=E2=80=
=9D =E2=80=94A sports media and marketing executive

On the possible ACC implosion: =E2=80=9CIf Miami and Virginia Tech would ge=
t their shit together and get back to competing for national championships,=
 along with Clemson and Florida State, it would help a ton. Commissioner Ji=
m Phillips is a nice guy, but I question whether a former Northwestern A.D.=
 is prepared to negotiate media deals worth billions, or have the vision to=
 anticipate what is next? The courts may decide it all.=E2=80=9D =E2=80=94A=
 Varsity subscriber

On my new favorite word: =E2=80=9CGrinfuck! Love it. Such a great, helpful =
newsletter.=E2=80=9D =E2=80=94A broadcaster

See you Thursday,
John

br/><br/>Earlier this month, I traveled to New York, where I conducted the =

ollar price
<i>is</i> causing angst in Bristol, which also recently observed Apple agre=

ontract at the end of next season. I=E2=80=99d expect ESPN boss <b>Jimmy Pi=

nel is nominated for the J.V.</i>: I received a text message from a senior =

break-glass situation.</li><br/><li><i>Khan=E2=80=99s Netflix prediction</i=
>: Earnings calls are generally terrible, filled with so much corporate pab=

 Netflix-NFL deal, I was reminded of Khan=E2=80=99s 1Q22 call, wherein he p=
resaged the present moment. <br/><br/>At the time, Khan said: =E2=80=9CNetf=
lix is willing to make adjustments and reverse positions when it identifies=
 an issue. A few years ago, Netflix put out on social that it was okay with=
 password-sharing amongst users. Obviously, that has changed. As recently a=
s 2021, Netflix's most senior executives said there would not be an ad =
product on Netflix. [To] quote from the Netflix earnings call just a year a=

advertising-free. Sports tends to be live and packed with advertising. So, =
there=E2=80=99s not a lot of synergies in that way.=E2=80=99 As we had ment=
ioned on our last earnings call, it's just a matter of time before Netf=
lix goes with live.=E2=80=9D</li><br/><li><i>NCAA athletes=E2=80=99 billion=
-dollar blitz</i>: This coming week could mark a pivotal moment for college=
 sports as the NCAA board of governors convenes on Thursday to discuss pote=
ntially settling three major antitrust lawsuits from athletes over compensa=
tion. The proposed deal, as reported by various media outlets, is extremely=
 ambitious: a $2.77 <i>billion</i> payout to former college athletes for pa=
st damages, plus a new revenue-sharing system aimed at present and future a=
thletes. Smaller conferences and schools are already grimacing at their sha=
re of the cost, possibly obscuring a more critical question: What exactly a=
re they getting in return?<br/><br/>Well, the end of those lawsuits, for on=
e. But what NCAA president <b>Charlie
Baker</b> <i>really</i> craves is stability, which has eluded the organizat=
ion ever since the Supreme Court rejected amateurism as an acceptable shiel=
d against antitrust laws. Since the unanimous decision in the landmark <i>N=
CAA v.</i> <i>Alston </i>case three years ago, the organization has been em=
broiled in incessant litigation, and it surely hopes that striking a deal w=
ith legal foe <b>Jeffrey Kessler</b> will alleviate the headaches. <br/><br=
/>Alas, a settlement alone won=E2=80=99t ensure immunity from new antitrust=

=80=9D whereby athletes will have an annual opportunity to express any grie=
vances before a judge. Alas, I know many in the antitrust field who are ske=
ptical at best, and dubious at worst, that this will be effective in curtai=
ling future litigation. With billions buying a semblance of peace in the co=
llege sports community, perhaps
it will simply pave the way for something with more teeth=E2=80=94a new fed=
eral law, perhaps, or collective bargaining. Time will tell. =E2=80=94<i>Er=
iq Gardner</i></li><br/><li><i>James Dolan=E2=80=99s defense</i>: Will the =

=2EO. <b>James Dolan</b>, whose Knicks=20=

 affirmed.) Now, Wigdor is pointing to developments at the U.S. Supreme Cou=

          <td style=3D"padding-bottom: 36px; color: #000000; font-size: 16p=
x; line-height: 30px; margin-top: 0; margin-bottom: 0;">Under <b>Sean McMan=
us</b>=E2=80=99s nearly three-decade tenure, CBS Sports was known for stabi=
lity, boasting the longest and deepest relationships in the business. And a=
s I=E2=80=99ve noted in the past, this was also the <i>leitmotif</i> of McM=
anus=E2=80=99s drama-free succession plan. <b>David Berson</b>, who ascende=
d to the top job last month after his old boss=E2=80=99s retirement, had be=
en McManus=E2=80=99s heir apparent for some 13 years, fully ensconced in on=
e of the industry=E2=80=99s great partnerships.<br/><br/>But as Berson prep=
ared for his first town hall meeting as president and C.E.O., he realized t=
hat this stability now seemed uncertain. Staffers were understandably anxio=
us about <b>Shari Redstone</b>=E2=80=99s enigmatic auction process for Para=
mount Global, the future of the linear business, and the increasingly exorb=
itant market for live sports rights. During a one-hour
presentation, Berson reminded his 600 or so charges that they=E2=80=99d nee=
d to remain focused on what they could control while staying aggressive in =
the rights landscape. =E2=80=9CThe message was continuity and stability, bu=
t with eyes wide open on all the challenges that we all face as part of thi=
s changing industry,=E2=80=9D Berson told me about two weeks later, over lu=
nch at a quiet Italian restaurant on West 57th Street, his first interview =
in his new job. =E2=80=9CWe have all of our marquee rights locked up for a =
long time. But the fans of today consume stuff very differently from the fa=
ns of yesterday, so we need to make sure that our brand and our content is =
as relevant for those fans moving forward as it has always been.=E2=80=9D<b=
r/><br/>I=E2=80=99ve known Berson for about a quarter-century, starting whe=
n he was a grunt at ESPN. He=E2=80=99s always been a pensive and thoughtful=
 guy, eschewing the limelight, and always happy to defer credit and attenti=
on. Last week, industry insiders took note when Berson
allowed E.V.P. <b>Dan Weinberg </b>to be quoted in news articles about the =
NFL schedule. In the old days, McManus believed CBS Sports should speak wit=
h one voice, and he had always been that voice.<br/><br/>Obviously, the def=
ining story around Paramount these days is about whether Redstone will sell=
 to <b>David Ellison</b> and RedBird or Apollo and Sony, or some other myst=
ery bidder. But when I pressed Berson on this topic <i>multiple times</i>=
=E2=80=94and, in particular, the difficulty of leading a sports group amid =
so much chaos at the parentco level=E2=80=94he elegantly demurred. Instead,=
 he focused on the strength of CBS Sports, which is locked into long-term d=
eals with the NFL, Big Ten, March Madness, UEFA, PGA Tour, and the Masters.=
 Despite the angst surrounding Shari=E2=80=99s next move, CBS produced the =
most watched program in television history with February=E2=80=99s Super Bo=
wl; racked up the largest NFL audience in 25 years; and broadcast the Final=
 Four, the Masters and the PGA
Championship. =E2=80=9COur folks understand the importance of what they do,=
 and people have focused on what we can control and the important role our =
content plays in the company and for sports fans,=E2=80=9D Berson said.<br/=
><br/>It=E2=80=99s a message that resonates throughout the network. Sources=
 have told me that the length of CBS Sports=E2=80=99 NFL deal=E2=80=94it ha=
s a decade left, although the NFL can opt out in seven years=E2=80=94is one=
 of the predominant reasons investors are kicking the tires on Paramount.<b=
r/></td>

x; line-height: 30px; margin-top: 0; margin-bottom: 0;">Berson finds it ext=
raordinarily difficult to discuss even small changes that will come now tha=
t he=E2=80=99s in charge of CBS Sports. Berson and McManus remain close, an=
d he is careful not to reveal areas where he disagreed even slightly with h=
is former boss. In truth, though, Berson bought into McManus=E2=80=99s worl=
d view on sports media and insists that he will carry it forward. Under McM=
anus, CBS focused on producing marquee events, opting to stay away from ran=
dom regular season games, and Berson doesn=E2=80=99t plan to move off that =
strategy. =E2=80=9CThat=E2=80=99s what CBS Sports has been for generations,=
=E2=80=9D Berson told me. =E2=80=9CWe=E2=80=99re very, very focused on big =
events that cut through and matter and deliver massive audiences, and our r=
ights portfolio is aligned to deliver on that.=E2=80=9D<br/><br/>You=E2=80=
=99re most likely to see Berson=E2=80=99s influence in digital and social, =
where CBS
Sports will take a few more risks, display more of a personality, and promo=
te a stronger CBS Sports-branded voice. You=E2=80=99ll even see it come out=
 in some of the music and graphics the network uses. And we=E2=80=99ve alre=
ady seen <i>some </i>intimation of his taste and perspective via his decisi=
on not to not renew the contracts of <b>Boomer Esiason</b> and <b>Phil Simm=
s</b>, longtime mainstays on <i>The NFL Today</i>. In their place, Berson b=
rought in <b>Matt Ryan</b> and renewed star newcomer from last year <b>J.J.=
 Watt</b>. I suggested that these moves bore his signature, but Berson repl=
ied that the show would have undergone some changes even if McManus were st=
ill around.<br/><br/>Continuity with change=E2=80=94that old spoofy Selina =
Meyer slogan from <i>Veep</i>=E2=80=94is an apt description of the Berson h=
ousekeeping remit. He replaced two legendary quarterbacks with a pair of yo=
unger retired All Pros and is leaving the rest of the roundtable in place. =
<b>Nate Burleson</b> even emerged
during the middle of the town hall to interview Berson onstage, playfully r=
ibbing him about his sartorial choices (those quarter zips) and his avuncul=
ar command of Gen Z tastes (he kidded him about his overemphasized pronunci=
ation of TikTok). =E2=80=9CWe really just talked about having a standard of=
 excellence in everything we do,=E2=80=9D Berson said. =E2=80=9CMy strong h=
ope and desire is for us to continue to push to get better and take chances=
=2E We want quality and we want risk.=20=
And I want to foster a culture that=E2=
=80=99s about being respectful and being classy and being good teammates wh=
ile setting a high bar.=E2=80=9D<br/></td>

x; line-height: 30px; margin-top: 0; margin-bottom: 0;"><i>On the NBA negot=
iations</i>: =E2=80=9C<b>Adam Silver</b> is smart and knows a 40-year relat=
ionship shouldn't just be tossed aside without letting the incumbent tr=
y to think of any angle possible before they move on. Plus, who knows where=
 <b>Zaz </b>could end up five years from now. Adam is in no rush to have to=
 call out the winners. Only the press is.=E2=80=9D =E2=80=94<i>A former med=
ia executive</i><br/><br/><i>More on the NBA negotiations</i>: =E2=80=9CMy =
co-workers and I were having a debate that I=E2=80=99d love for you to weig=
h in on. Is <i>Inside the NBA</i> a good basketball show, or is it cable=E2=
=80=99s most successful late night show? My co-worker feels it=E2=80=99s no=
t truly a basketball show because you don=E2=80=99t walk away from it knowi=
ng more about the game. I argue it=E2=80=99s a basketball show, but that it=
=E2=80=99s built for a midnight/1 a.m. audience that is used to being enter=
tained
as much as taught.=E2=80=9D =E2=80=94<i>A Varsity subscriber</i><br/>[<i>Ed=
 note: If I only get those two choices, I=E2=80=99ll agree with your co-wor=
ker. It=E2=80=99s an entertainment show. In reality, it falls right in betw=
een, which is why it=E2=80=99s been so hard for ESPN to figure out how to c=
ompete with it.</i>]<br/><br/><i>On the NFL and Netflix</i>: =E2=80=9CNetfl=
ix=E2=80=99s NFL deal has nothing to do with acquiring subs or mitigating c=
hurn domestically. It has everything to do with wising up on the R.O.I. of =
their content. <b>Matt Belloni</b> had <i>The Irishman </i>production budge=
t at $225M, exclusive of marketing, so let's say $300M. Two NFL games f=
or half the price of a shitty movie is objectively a good deal. =E2=80=A6 A=
nd most of that return will come from ads. Netflix comfortably has scale ov=
er everyone in terms of subs and time spent. But ~all of their subs are ad-=
free! Live sports unlocks all of that because it allows them to serve the =
=E2=80=98ad-free=E2=80=99 members with ads=E2=80=94pre- and postgame, timeo=
uts,
halftime=E2=80=94and it carries a traditional B2B benefit, too. They can bu=
ndle the ad sales like the old days=E2=80=94<i>Sure, Liberty Insurance, you=
 want a spot on our Christmas Day game? Well, also buy X, Y, and Z!</i>=E2=
=80=9D =E2=80=94<i>A data analyst</i><br/><br/><i>More on the NFL and Netfl=
ix</i>: =E2=80=9CHave you heard anything about who=E2=80=99s handling produ=
ction for the NFL Netflix Christmas games?=E2=80=9D =E2=80=94<i>A productio=
n company executive who wants business.</i><br/>[<i>Ed note: This is one of=
 the great unanswered questions from the Netflix-NFL deal</i>. <i>I=E2=80=
=99m working on it.</i>]<br/><br/><i>Still more on the NFL and Netflix</i>:=
 =E2=80=9CWith Netflix acquiring Christmas Day NFL games, I=E2=80=99m curio=
us how this will shake out for commercial video customers.=E2=80=9D =E2=80=
=94<i>A digital content and social media executive</i><br/>[<i>Ed note: Thi=
s is another of the great unanswered questions from the Netflix-NFL deal</i=
>.]<br/><br/><i>On NFL media rights</i>: =E2=80=9CRegarding your insight in=
to the NFL=E2=80=99s future
TV/streaming rights deal a decade from now: It will no doubt <i>not</i> be =
in a decade, when the current deal expires after 2033, but rather when the =
NFL undoubtedly decides to opt out early in seven years. The legacy compani=
es like Fox and CBS will find their current deal a bargain compared to the =
ransom they will have to pay once that opt-out is triggered.=E2=80=9D =E2=
=80=94<i>A broadcaster</i><br/><br/><i>Everyone is an editor</i>: =E2=80=9C=
I=E2=80=99d love to see more coverage on the slow-motion car wreck that is =
the PGA/PIF negotiations. It=E2=80=99s quite bad, probably worse than repor=
ted. No major sport, especially one whose audience is aging faster than the=
 others, has done more damage to its brand in such a short time than men=E2=
=80=99s professional golf. The fact that the players, who are smart men but=
 clearly in over their heads, are driving the decision is insane. There=E2=
=80=99s a reason the NBA didn=E2=80=99t ask <b>LeBron</b> to negotiate its =
rights deals. It=E2=80=99s a huge issue, or as your eloquent editor
<b>Jon Kelly </b>likes to say, it=E2=80=99s <i>one of the stories of our ti=
me</i>.=E2=80=9D =E2=80=94<i>A sports media and marketing executive</i><br/=
><br/><i>On the possible ACC implosion</i>:<i> =E2=80=9C</i>If Miami and Vi=
rginia Tech would get their shit together and get back to competing for nat=
ional championships, along with Clemson and Florida State, it would help a =
ton. Commissioner <b>Jim Phillips </b>is a nice guy, but I question whether=
 a former Northwestern A.D. is prepared to negotiate media deals worth bill=
ions, or have the vision to anticipate what is next? The courts may decide =
it all.=E2=80=9D =E2=80=94<i>A Varsity subscriber</i><br/><br/><i>On my new=
 favorite word</i>: =E2=80=9CGrinfuck! Love it. Such a great, helpful newsl=
etter.=E2=80=9D =E2=80=94<i>A broadcaster</i><br/></td>
